raw_case:
Abdominal mouse: Floating intra-abdominal cystic lymphatic malformation – An exceedingly rare case report


Introduction and importance

Lymphangiomas are rare benign lymphatic malformations, typically affecting the head, neck, or axillary regions, with abdominal cystic lymphatic malformations (CLM) being particularly uncommon in adults. Abdominal CLM account for less than 5 % of all lymphangiomas and 7 % of all abdominal cystic lesions in adults with a prevalence of approximately 1 in 250,000. These lesions are commonly found by accident during imaging investigations and are asymptomatic. Being free of any attachment, floating in the peritoneal cavity make it unique in its presentation.

Case presentation

We report a case of 25-year-old female who complaining of right upper abdominal pain. Initial laboratory and imaging studies by abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan showed multiple complex cysts with undetermined origin. Exploration laparoscopy was performed, revealing over 50 variable-sized cysts, some of them were floating freely within the abdominal cavity. Complete resection of all cysts was performed. Postoperative recovery was uneventful, and histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of cystic lymphangiomas.

Clinical discussion

This case demonstrates the unusual presentation of multiple, floating, and variably sized intra-abdominal cystic lymphangiomas, a rare finding that presents significant diagnostic and management challenges. The absence of attachment to common intra-abdominal structures, such as the mesentery or omentum, further complicates the clinical picture.

Conclusion

Abdominal CLM must be considered as possible diagnosis especially if the cysts are multiple and complex. Freely floating abdominal CLM may cause the symptoms of a moving lump described by the patient, which must be taken in consideration.

Highlights

•Cystic lymphangiomas are uncommon lymphatic malformation developed during embryological period.•Floating lymphangiomas are significantly rare type of cystic lymphangioma and has not been reported in any other case.•Intra-abdominal lymphangiomas have unspecific clinical manifestation that challenges the diagnosis.•Therapeutic modalities of intra-abdominal lymphangiomas are variable depend on the case.1

Introduction

Lymphangiomas are benign, rare lymphatic malformations characterized by thin-walled cystic lesions. Most of them are located in the head and neck region (75 %), the axillary region (20 %), and, less than 5 % of cases, the intra-abdominal region. Furthermore, most cystic lymphatic malformation (CLM) is diagnosed in childhood and are rarely discovered in adulthood [[1], [2], [3]].Intra-abdominal CLM account for 7 % of all abdominal cystic lesions in adults, with a very rare prevalence of about 1 in 250,000 people [4]. They are found in the mesentery, greater omentum, and retroperitoneum. However, most cases are asymptomatic and identified incidentally by imaging modalities such as CT scans or ultrasound [3]. Nevertheless, a definitive diagnosis by biopsy should be undertaken after the surgical removal of cysts to optimize the management plan and prognosis. On the other hand, intra-abdominal CLM can present with symptoms of bowel obstruction, including abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting [5,6].However, our case presents with multiple, floating, and variably sized intra-abdominal CLM not attached to the mesentery, greater omentum, mesocolon, or retroperitoneum. This unusual and interesting finding is novel and, as far as we know, has not been reported in any other case.2

Case presentation

A 25-year-old female admitted to our hospital, referred from her obstetrician, complaining of dull aching right upper abdominal pain for 2 months of duration. Her past medical and surgical history was unremarkable. No associated symptoms, such as recent changes in bowel habits, diarrhea, or association with fatty meal, were reported. Vital signs were normal. Abdominal examination showed upper abdominal tenderness without guarding or rigidity, and a mobile lump inside her abdomen.Routine laboratory test was unremarkable, including pancreatic and liver enzymes. Ultrasonography showed large, multiple cysts from pelvis to sub-hepatic region of undetermined origin. Contrast-enhanced CT scan showed (Fig. 1) multiple complex cysts, possibly of ovarian origin. Therefore, we performed pelvic ultrasound with no evidence of ovarian cystic lesion.During an exploratory laparoscopy, whole abdomen and pelvis were explored; multiple variable-sized cysts were observed throughout the abdominal cavity, ranging from 1 cm to 10 cm in size. Notably, the ovaries appeared normal, as confirmed by laparoscopy and imaging studies. Due to the presence of these cysts in different locations and their size diversity, we decided to perform an upper midline open laparotomy to resect the large cysts.These cysts were either found floating freely (Video 1) or attached to the transverse colon mesentery, sub-hepatic area and greater omentum, right paracolic gutter (Video 2), retrocecal region, pelvis, and near the stomach and duodenum. Intraoperative period was uneventful with successful removal of cystic lesion. There was no need for bowel resection as the cysts were only loosely attached to the mesentery, not to the bowel itself. Over 50 cysts were removed (Fig. 2) and sent for histopathology, which showed multilocular cysts, covered by flat endothelium containing collection of lymphoid tissues, foam cells, and smooth muscle cells in the wall of the cysts, confirming the diagnosis of intra-abdominal CLM.On postoperative day 2, patient's course was uneventful, so she was discharged home with follow-up plan. 3 months later, the patient was in good health and was able to resume her daily life activities with no persistence or recurrence of the symptoms.3

Discussion

Lymphangiomas are rare benign fluid-filled cystic malformations of lymphatic vessels. The cause of lymphangiomas is related to abnormal lymphatic system development during fetal embryology. This occurs when primary lymphatic sacs fail to fuse properly with the main lymphatic system. Some may be acquired, resulting from inflammation, trauma or lymphatic obstruction. Despite this, the exact cause of cystic lymphangiomas is still not clearly understood. [1,7].Abdominal CLM are a very rare type of lymphangioma, accounting for less than 5 % of all cases. More than 80 % of lymphangioma cases are diagnosed within the first few years of life [1,3]. Intra-abdominal CLM are mostly attached to intra-abdominal structures, including the mesentery, greater omentum, mesocolon, and retroperitoneum [3,4,8]. A few cases of abdominal CLM have reported cystic lesions in the pancreas [9] and stomach [10]. However, in contrast to these studies [3,4,9,11,12], a floating isolated intra-abdominal cyst was incidentally noted by laparoscopy during the operation in our case. Moreover, the presence of multiple variable-sized cystic lesions in distributed locations, which is a very rare finding according to the literature, makes the diagnosis of abdominal CLM challenging.Preoperative diagnosis of abdominal CLM is complex and requires a high degree of clinical suspicion. In addition to being asymptomatic [3,5], the clinical features of abdominal CLM vary significantly depending on size and location, including symptoms such as abdominal pain, mass, and bowel obstruction [3,6,11]. In our case, the patient presented with vague, unspecified abdominal pain without any characteristic signs on examination, making the diagnosis challenging.Beyond clinical manifestations, radiological findings using abdominal ultrasound, CT scan, or magnetic resonance imaging are important for diagnosing abdominal CLM. Additionally, these imaging modalities help determine the location, size, and association of abdominal CLM with surrounding organs, which are essential for proper surgical management. However, histopathological examination of a biopsy remains the definitive diagnosis.The management of abdominal CLM is variable and includes multiple treatment modalities depending on abdominal CLM features and patient symptoms, including surgical and conservative treatment. As some abdominal CLMs spontaneously regress [4], asymptomatic patients are usually managed conservatively with imaging and clinical follow-up. On the other hand, symptomatic patients may require percutaneous intervention, such as aspiration or sclerotherapy, or surgical intervention, including laparoscopy or open laparotomy [3,4]. Despite the treatment modality used, complete removal of abdominal CLM should be achieved due to the significant risk of relapse, which can reach 100 % according to previous studies [4]. Despite the high rate of recurrence and injuries to surrounding tissue reported in previous studies, we achieved a successful, uncomplicated complete resection of multiple intra-abdominal cystic lymphangiomas.4

Conclusion

Despite being rare, abdominal CLM must be considered as possible diagnosis especially if the cysts are multiple and complex. Being without attachment and freely floating in the abdominal cavity, abdominal CLM may cause the symptoms of a moving lump described by the patient, which must be taken in consideration.5

Method

This work has been reported in accordance with the SCARE guidelines [13].The following are the supplementary data related to this article.

Video 1

The intra-operative laparoscopic video shows a freely floating cyst inside the abdomen.Video 1

Video 2

The intra-operative laparoscopic video shows intra-abdominal cysts attached to the transverse colon mesentery, subhepatic area, greater omentum, and right paracolic gutter.Video 2

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication and any accompanying images. The patient provided informed consent before their involvement in the study, acknowledging their understanding of the research procedures, risks, and confidentiality.

Ethical approval

As it's a case report, it is exempted from ethical approval by local institution responding on the case.

Funding

This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Author contribution

Hamza A. Abdul-Hafez, Fathi Milhem: data curation, writing-original draft preparation. Hamza A. Abdul-Hafez and Mohammed Hajhamad: supervision, writing-reviewing and editing.

Guarantor

Hamza A. Abdul-Hafez, Fathi Milhem, and Mohammed Hajhamad.

Conflict of interest statement

The authors have no conflict of interest.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 25-year-old female.
- Chief Complaint: Dull aching right upper abdominal pain for 2 months.
- History of Present Illness: Persistent right upper abdominal pain, not associated with changes in bowel habits, diarrhea, or fatty meals.
- Past Medical History: None.
- Personal History: None reported.
- Family History: None provided.
- Physical Examination: Upper abdominal tenderness with a mobile lump; no guarding or rigidity.
- Ancillary Tests:  
  - Routine laboratory tests: Normal, including pancreatic and liver enzymes.  
  - Ultrasonography: Multiple large, cystic structures from pelvis to sub-hepatic region, undetermined origin.  
  - Contrast-enhanced CT scan: Multiple complex cysts, initially thought to be of ovarian origin.  
  - Pelvic ultrasound: No ovarian cystic lesions.  
  - Histopathological examination: Multilocular cysts with flat endothelium, lymphatic tissue, foam cells, and smooth muscle cells, confirming intra-abdominal cystic lymphatic malformations (CLM).  
  - Surgical findings: Over 50 variable-sized cysts, some floating freely, others minimally attached to structures like the mesentery, greater omentum, and sub-hepatic area.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Possibilities Based on Imaging**:  
   - Ovarian cystic lesions were initially suspected as the CT scan suggested possible ovarian origin. However, pelvic ultrasound and laparoscopy ruled this out, as the ovaries appeared normal.
   - Other potential abdominal cystic lesions, such as mesenteric or retroperitoneal cystic masses, were considered.

2. **Consideration of Cystic Lymphatic Malformations (CLM)**:  
   - The presence of multiple, complex cysts suggested CLM.  
   - The cysts' unique presentation (freely floating and minimally attached) made this case more unusual but aligned with reported CLM variability.  
   - Definitively confirmed through histopathological evaluation of the resected cysts, showing characteristic lymphatic endothelial features.

3. **Exclusion of Other Disorders**:  
   - Bowel obstruction was excluded as there was no evidence of obstruction intraoperatively or clinically.  
   - Neoplastic conditions were ruled out during histopathological examination, as no malignant features were identified.

The diagnostic process highlighted the importance of imaging, intraoperative findings, and histopathological confirmation in identifying rare abdominal CLM.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Intra-abdominal cystic lymphatic malformations (CLM) presenting as multiple floating cystic lesions, some minimally attached to surrounding structures.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 25-year-old female presenting with right upper abdominal pain for 2 months. This is a subacute to chronic symptom.

<step 2> Initial investigations included routine lab tests which were normal, ruling out acute inflammation or significant liver/pancreatic dysfunction as the primary cause of pain based on standard enzyme markers.

<step 3> Ultrasonography revealed multiple large cystic structures, indicating a cystic mass in the abdomen, but the origin was undetermined initially.

<step 4> A contrast-enhanced CT scan further characterized these cysts as complex and initially suggested an ovarian origin, which is a common differential for cystic masses in women, especially in the pelvic and lower abdominal regions.

<step 5> A pelvic ultrasound was performed to specifically evaluate the ovaries, and it revealed no ovarian cystic lesions. This effectively rules out the ovaries as the source of the cystic masses, necessitating a reconsideration of the origin of these cysts.

<step 6> Histopathological examination of the cystic structures was performed. The findings of multilocular cysts with flat endothelium, lymphatic tissue, foam cells, and smooth muscle cells are pathognomonic for cystic lymphatic malformations (CLM). This provides a definitive diagnosis at the microscopic level.

<step 7> Surgical findings corroborated the diagnosis of CLM, revealing numerous cysts of varying sizes distributed within the abdomen, attached to or free-floating near structures like the mesentery, omentum, and sub-hepatic area. This macroscopic observation is consistent with the imaging and histological diagnosis.

<step 8> Integrating all the findings – the patient's presentation of abdominal pain, the imaging revealing complex cystic masses of non-ovarian origin, the histopathological confirmation of lymphatic malformation, and the surgical findings – strongly points towards a final diagnosis of intra-abdominal cystic lymphatic malformations.

### Answer:
Intra-abdominal Cystic Lymphatic Malformations (CLM)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a 25-year-old female presenting with right upper abdominal pain for 2 months. This is a subacute to chronic symptom.

Initial investigations included routine lab tests which were normal, ruling out acute inflammation or significant liver/pancreatic dysfunction as the primary cause of pain based on standard enzyme markers.
Ultrasonography revealed multiple large cystic structures, indicating a cystic mass in the abdomen, but the origin was undetermined initially.

A contrast-enhanced CT scan further characterized these cysts as complex and initially suggested an ovarian origin, which is a common differential for cystic masses in women, especially in the pelvic and lower abdominal regions.

A pelvic ultrasound was performed to specifically evaluate the ovaries, and it revealed no ovarian cystic lesions. This effectively rules out the ovaries as the source of the cystic masses, necessitating a reconsideration of the origin of these cysts.

Histopathological examination of the cystic structures was performed. The findings of multilocular cysts with flat endothelium, lymphatic tissue, foam cells, and smooth muscle cells are pathognomonic for cystic lymphatic malformations (CLM). This provides a definitive diagnosis at the microscopic level.

Surgical findings corroborated the diagnosis of CLM, revealing numerous cysts of varying sizes distributed within the abdomen, attached to or free-floating near structures like the mesentery, omentum, and sub-hepatic area. This macroscopic observation is consistent with the imaging and histological diagnosis.

Integrating all the findings – the patient's presentation of abdominal pain, the imaging revealing complex cystic masses of non-ovarian origin, the histopathological confirmation of lymphatic malformation, and the surgical findings – strongly points towards a final diagnosis of intra-abdominal cystic lymphatic malformations.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Ovarian cystic lesions were initially suspected based on CT scan findings, but were ruled out by pelvic ultrasound and laparoscopy, leading to consideration of other abdominal cystic lesions like mesenteric or retroperitoneal cystic masses.

<Step 2> Multiple, complex cysts suggested cystic lymphatic malformations (CLM), with their unique presentation (freely floating and minimally attached) being more unusual but consistent with reported CLM variability.
<Step 3> CLM was definitively confirmed through histopathological evaluation of the resected cysts, which showed characteristic lymphatic endothelial features.
<Step 4> Bowel obstruction was excluded as there was no evidence of obstruction intraoperatively or clinically.
<Step 5> Neoplastic conditions were ruled out during histopathological examination, as no malignant features were identified.
<Step 6> The diagnostic process emphasized the importance of imaging, intraoperative findings, and histopathological confirmation in identifying rare abdominal CLM.
<Step 7> Final diagnosis: Intra-abdominal cystic lymphatic malformations (CLM) presenting as multiple floating cystic lesions, some minimally attached to surrounding structures.
